title: John the Baptist

infobox.value: c. 6 BC[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Herodian Kingdom of Judea](/wiki/Herodian_Kingdom_of_Judea "Herodian Kingdom of Judea"), [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire")

c. AD 30[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Machaerus](/wiki/Machaerus "Machaerus"), [Province of Judaea](/wiki/Judaea_(Roman_Province) "Judaea (Roman Province)"), Roman Empire

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (all [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") which [venerate saints](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration")), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"), [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") faith,[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Mandaeism](/wiki/Mandaeism "Mandaeism")

* [Church of Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/Church_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist,_Ein_Karem,_Jerusalem "Church of Saint John the Baptist, Ein Karem, Jerusalem") in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") * [Umayyad Mosque](/wiki/Umayyad_Mosque "Umayyad Mosque") in [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") * [Nabi Yahya Mosque](/wiki/Nabi_Yahya_Mosque "Nabi Yahya Mosque") in [Sebastia](/wiki/Sebastia,_Nablus "Sebastia, Nablus") * [Aachen Cathedral](/wiki/Aachen_Cathedral "Aachen Cathedral") in [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), Germany

* 24 June ([Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_St._John_the_Baptist "Nativity of St. John the Baptist")) * 29 August ([Beheading](/wiki/Beheading_of_John_the_Baptist "Beheading of John the Baptist")) * 7 January ([Synaxis](/wiki/Synaxis "Synaxis")—[Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite")) * 30 [Paoni](/wiki/Paoni "Paoni") ([Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_St._John_the_Baptist "Nativity of St. John the Baptist")—[Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria")) * 2 [Thout](/wiki/Thout "Thout") ([Beheading](/wiki/Beheading_of_John_the_Baptist "Beheading of John the Baptist")—Coptic) * 1 [Hitia](/wiki/Hitia "Hitia") ([Birthday](/wiki/Dehwa_Daimana "Dehwa Daimana")—[Mandaean](/wiki/Mandaeism "Mandaeism"))
Camel-skin robe, cross, lamb, scroll with words *Ecce [Agnus Dei](/wiki/Agnus_Dei "Agnus Dei")-*, platter with own head, pouring water from hands or [scallop](/wiki/Scallop "Scallop") shell

in [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") and [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy"), **Saint John the Immerser** in the [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") tradition,[[21]](#cite_note-22) and as the prophet **[Yahya ibn Zakariya](/wiki/Yahya_ibn_Zakariya "Yahya ibn Zakariya")** in [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). He is sometimes referred to as **John the Baptiser**.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1st-century prophet

Not to be confused with [John the Apostle](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle").

"Saint John the Baptist" redirects here. For other uses, see [Saint John the Baptist (disambiguation)](/wiki/Saint_John_the_Baptist_(disambiguation) "Saint John the Baptist (disambiguation)").

**John the Baptist**[[note 1]](#cite_note-18) (c. 6 BC[[18]](#cite_note-19) – c. AD 30) was a [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") preacher active in the area of the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") in the early first century AD.[[19]](#cite_note-Oxford_University_Press2005-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) He is also known as **Saint John the Forerunner** in [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") and [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy"), **Saint John the Immerser** in the [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") tradition,[[21]](#cite_note-22) and as the prophet **[Yahya ibn Zakariya](/wiki/Yahya_ibn_Zakariya "Yahya ibn Zakariya")** in [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). He is sometimes referred to as **John the Baptiser**.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

John is mentioned by the [Roman Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_the_Roman_Empire "History of the Jews in the Roman Empire") historian [Josephus](/wiki/Josephus "Josephus"),[[25]](#cite_note-26) and he is revered as a major religious figure in Christianity,[[26]](#cite_note-27) Islam, the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"),[[27]](#cite_note-Compilations1983-28) the [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") faith, and [Mandaeism](/wiki/Mandaeism "Mandaeism"); in the last of these he is considered to be the final and most vital prophet. He is considered to be a [prophet](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet") of [God](/wiki/God_in_Abrahamic_religions "God in Abrahamic religions") by all of the aforementioned faiths, and is honoured as a [saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint") in many [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"). According to the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), John anticipated a [messianic figure](/wiki/Messianic_figure "Messianic figure") greater than himself;[[28]](#cite_note-29) in the [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel"), he is portrayed as the precursor or forerunner of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus").[[29]](#cite_note-30) According to the [Gospel of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), Jesus himself identifies John as "Elijah who is to come",[[30]](#cite_note-31) which is a direct reference to the [Book of Malachi](/wiki/Book_of_Malachi "Book of Malachi") (Malachi 4:5),[[31]](#cite_note-32) as confirmed by the angel [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel"), who announced John's birth to his father [Zechariah](/wiki/Zechariah_(New_Testament_figure) "Zechariah (New Testament figure)").[[32]](#cite_note-33) According to the [Gospel of Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke"), John and Jesus were relatives.[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Some scholars think that John belonged to the [Essenes](/wiki/Essenes "Essenes"), a semi-[ascetic](/wiki/Ascetic "Ascetic") Jewish sect who expected a messiah and practised ritual [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism").[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37) John used baptism as the central symbol or [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament")[[37]](#cite_note-38) of his pre-messianic movement. Most biblical scholars agree that John [baptized Jesus](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus"),[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) and several New Testament accounts report that some of Jesus's early followers had previously been followers of John.[[40]](#cite_note-41) According to the New Testament, John was sentenced to death and [subsequently beheaded](/wiki/Beheading_of_John_the_Baptist "Beheading of John the Baptist") by [Herod Antipas](/wiki/Herod_Antipas "Herod Antipas") around AD 30 after John rebuked him for divorcing his wife and then unlawfully wedding [Herodias](/wiki/Herodias "Herodias"), the wife of his brother [Herod Philip I](/wiki/Herod_Philip_I "Herod Philip I"). Josephus also mentions John in the *[Antiquities of the Jews](/wiki/Antiquities_of_the_Jews "Antiquities of the Jews")* and states that he was executed by order of Herod Antipas in the fortress at [Machaerus](/wiki/Machaerus "Machaerus").[[41]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Pieter_Bruegel_the_Elder_-_The_Sermon_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist.jpg/500px-Pieter_Bruegel_the_Elder_-_The_Sermon_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pieter_Bruegel_the_Elder_-_The_Sermon_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist.jpg)

*The Preaching of St. John the Baptist* by [Pieter Bruegel the Elder](/wiki/Pieter_Bruegel_the_Elder "Pieter Bruegel the Elder"), 1566

Followers of John existed into the second century AD, and some proclaimed him to be the [Messiah awaited by Jews](/wiki/Messiah_in_Judaism "Messiah in Judaism").[[42]](#cite_note-43) In modern times, the followers of John the Baptist are the [Mandaeans](/wiki/Mandaeans "Mandaeans"), an ancient [ethnoreligious group](/wiki/Ethnoreligious_group "Ethnoreligious group") who believe that he is their greatest and final prophet.[[43]](#cite_note-Buckley-44)[[44]](#cite_note-Drower-45) In the [Roman martyrology](/wiki/Roman_martyrology "Roman martyrology"), John is the only saint whose birth and death are both commemorated.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

## Gospel narratives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Gospel narratives")]

See also: [Baptism of Jesus](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") and [Beheading of John the Baptist](/wiki/Beheading_of_John_the_Baptist "Beheading of John the Baptist")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Onorio_Marinari_Salom%C3%A9_con_la_cabeza_del_Bautista_1680_Minneapolis_MA.jpg/250px-Onorio_Marinari_Salom%C3%A9_con_la_cabeza_del_Bautista_1680_Minneapolis_MA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Onorio_Marinari_Salom%C3%A9_con_la_cabeza_del_Bautista_1680_Minneapolis_MA.jpg)

[Salome](/wiki/Salome "Salome") is given the severed head of John the Baptist. [Onorio Marinari](/wiki/Onorio_Marinari "Onorio Marinari"), 1670s.

John the Baptist is mentioned in all four canonical [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") and the non-canonical [Gospel of the Nazarenes](/wiki/Gospel_of_the_Nazarenes "Gospel of the Nazarenes"). The [Synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels") ([Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark"), [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke")) describe John baptising Jesus; in the [Gospel of John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") this is inferred by many to be referred to in John 1:32.[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### In Mark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: In Mark")]

The Gospel of Mark introduces John as a fulfilment of a prophecy from the [Book of Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah"), which is a conflation of texts from Isaiah, [Malachi](/wiki/Book_of_Malachi "Book of Malachi"), and [Exodus](/wiki/Book_of_Exodus "Book of Exodus"),[[48]](#cite_note-Carl_R-49) about a messenger being sent ahead, and a voice crying out in the wilderness. John is described as wearing clothes of camel's hair, and living on [locusts](/wiki/Locust "Locust") and wild [honey](/wiki/Honey "Honey"). John proclaims baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sin, and says another will come after him who will not baptize with water, but with the Holy Spirit.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Massimo_Stanzione_-_Beheading_of_St_John_the_Baptist_-_WGA21701.jpg/500px-Massimo_Stanzione_-_Beheading_of_St_John_the_Baptist_-_WGA21701.jpg)](/wiki/File:Massimo_Stanzione_-_Beheading_of_St_John_the_Baptist_-_WGA21701.jpg)

*Beheading of St John the Baptist* by [Massimo Stanzione](/wiki/Massimo_Stanzione "Massimo Stanzione"), 1635

Jesus comes to John, and is baptized by him in the River Jordan. The account describes how, as he emerges from the water, Jesus sees the heavens open and the Holy Spirit descends on him "like a dove", and he hears a voice from heaven that says, "You are my Son, the Beloved; with you I am well pleased".[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Later in the gospel there is an account of John's death. It is introduced by an incident where the Tetrarch [Herod Antipas](/wiki/Herod_Antipas "Herod Antipas"), hearing stories about Jesus, imagines that this is John the Baptist raised from the dead. It then explains that John had rebuked Herod for marrying [Herodias](/wiki/Herodias "Herodias"), the ex-wife of his brother, named here as Philip. Herodias demands his execution, but Herod, who "liked to listen" to John, is reluctant to do so because he fears him, knowing he is a "righteous and holy man".

The account then describes how Herodias' unnamed daughter dances before Herod, who is pleased and offers her anything she asks for in return. When the girl asks her mother what she should request, she is told to demand the head of John the Baptist. Reluctantly, Herod orders the beheading of John, and his head is delivered to her, at her request, on a plate. John's [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)") take the body away and bury it in a tomb.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

The Gospel refers to Antipas as "King"[[51]](#cite_note-52) and the ex-husband of Herodias is named as Philip, but he is known to have been called [Herod II](/wiki/Herod_II "Herod II").[[52]](#cite_note-Florence_Morgan_Gillman2003-53) Although the wording implies the girl was the daughter of Herodias, many texts describe her as "Herod's daughter, Herodias". Since these texts are early and significant and the reading is '[difficult](/wiki/Lectio_difficilior_potior "Lectio difficilior potior")', many scholars see this as the original version, altered in later versions and in Matthew and Luke.[[52]](#cite_note-Florence_Morgan_Gillman2003-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55) Josephus says that Herodias had a daughter by the name of Salome.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

Many scholars have seen the story of John arrested, executed, and buried in a tomb as a conscious foreshadowing of the fate of Jesus.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*]

### In Matthew

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In Matthew")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Mattia_Preti_-_San_Giovanni_Battista_Predicazione.jpg/250px-Mattia_Preti_-_San_Giovanni_Battista_Predicazione.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mattia_Preti_-_San_Giovanni_Battista_Predicazione.jpg)

*St. John the Baptist Preaching*, c. 1665, by [Mattia Preti](/wiki/Mattia_Preti "Mattia Preti")

The Gospel of Matthew account begins with the same modified quotation from Isaiah,[[57]](#cite_note-58) moving the Malachi and Exodus material to later in the text, where it is quoted by Jesus.[[58]](#cite_note-59) The description of John is possibly taken directly from Mark ("clothing of camel's hair with a leather belt around his waist, and his food was locusts and wild honey"), along with the proclamation that one was coming who would baptize with the Holy Spirit "and fire".[[59]](#cite_note-60) The book of Matthew next has Jesus coming to John to be baptized, but John objects because he is not worthy because Jesus is the one that brings the baptism in the Spirit.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

Unlike Mark, Matthew describes John as critical of [Pharisees](/wiki/Pharisees "Pharisees") and [Sadducees](/wiki/Sadducees "Sadducees")—calling them a "[brood of vipers](/wiki/Serpents_in_the_Bible#Gospels "Serpents in the Bible")"—and as preaching "the kingdom of heaven is at hand" and a "coming judgment".[[61]](#cite_note-62)

Matthew shortens the account of the beheading of John, and adds two elements: that Herod Antipas wants John dead, and that the death is reported to Jesus by his disciples.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Matthew's approach is to shift the focus away from Herod and onto John as a prototype of Jesus. Where Mark has Herod killing John reluctantly and at Herodias' insistence, Matthew describes him as wanting John dead.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

### In Luke and Acts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In Luke and Acts")]

Main article: [Nativity of John the Baptist](/wiki/Nativity_of_John_the_Baptist "Nativity of John the Baptist")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Piero%2C_battesimo_di_cristo_04.jpg/250px-Piero%2C_battesimo_di_cristo_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piero,_battesimo_di_cristo_04.jpg)

*[The Baptism of Jesus Christ](/wiki/The_Baptism_of_Christ_(Piero_della_Francesca) "The Baptism of Christ (Piero della Francesca)")* by Piero della Francesca, c. 1448–1450

The [Gospel of Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") adds an account of John's infancy, introducing him as the miraculous son of [Zechariah](/wiki/Zechariah_(priest) "Zechariah (priest)"), an old priest, and his wife [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_(biblical_figure) "Elizabeth (biblical figure)"), who was past [menopause](/wiki/Menopause "Menopause") and therefore unable to have children.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67) According to this account, the birth of John was foretold by the angel [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel") to Zechariah while he was performing his functions as a priest in the temple of Jerusalem. Since he is described as a priest of the course of [Abijah](/wiki/Abijah "Abijah") and Elizabeth as one of the [daughters of Aaron](/wiki/Daughters_of_Aaron "Daughters of Aaron"),[[67]](#cite_note-68) this would make John a descendant of [Aaron](/wiki/Aaron "Aaron") on both his father's and mother's side.[[68]](#cite_note-69) On the basis of this account, the Catholic as well as the Anglican and Lutheran liturgical calendars placed the feast of the Nativity of John the Baptist on 24 June, six months before Christmas.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

Elizabeth is described as a "relative" of Mary the mother of Jesus, in Luke 1:36.[[70]](#cite_note-71) There is no mention of a family relationship between John and Jesus in the other Gospels, and [Raymond E. Brown](/wiki/Raymond_E._Brown "Raymond E. Brown") has described it as "of dubious [historicity](/wiki/Historicity "Historicity")".[[71]](#cite_note-72) [Géza Vermes](/wiki/G%C3%A9za_Vermes "Géza Vermes") has called it "artificial and undoubtedly Luke's creation".[[72]](#cite_note-73) The many similarities between Luke's story of the birth of John and the Old Testament account of the birth of [Samuel](/wiki/Samuel_(Biblical_figure)#Family "Samuel (Biblical figure)") suggest that Luke's account of the annunciation and birth of Jesus are modelled on that of Samuel.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

Uniquely in the Gospel of Luke, John explicitly teaches charity, baptizes tax-collectors, and advises soldiers. He teaches his disciples to pray.[[74]](#cite_note-75) [Frederic Farrar](/wiki/Frederic_Farrar "Frederic Farrar") notes that John travels around the Jordan region to reach people, whereas in the accounts in the other gospels, the crowds come to him.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

The text briefly mentions that John is imprisoned and later beheaded by Herod, but the Gospel of Luke lacks the story of a step-daughter dancing for Herod and requesting John's head.

The [Book of Acts](/wiki/Book_of_Acts "Book of Acts") portrays some disciples of John becoming followers of Jesus,[[76]](#cite_note-77) a development not reported by the gospels except for the early case of [Andrew](/wiki/Saint_Andrew "Saint Andrew"), Simon Peter's brother.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### In the Gospel of John

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In the Gospel of John")]

The fourth gospel describes John the Baptist as "a man sent from God" who "was not the light", but "came as a witness, to bear witness to the light, so that through him everyone might believe".[[78]](#cite_note-79) John confirms that he is not the Christ nor Elijah nor 'the prophet' when asked by Jewish priests and Pharisees; instead, he described himself as the "voice of one crying in the wilderness".[[79]](#cite_note-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Mathis_Gothart_Gr%C3%BCnewald_024.jpg/250px-Mathis_Gothart_Gr%C3%BCnewald_024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mathis_Gothart_Gr%C3%BCnewald_024.jpg)

[Matthias Grünewald](/wiki/Matthias_Gr%C3%BCnewald "Matthias Grünewald"), detail of the *[Isenheim Altarpiece](/wiki/Isenheim_Altarpiece "Isenheim Altarpiece")*

Jesus's baptism is implied but not depicted. Unlike the other gospels, it is John himself who testifies to seeing "the Spirit come down from heaven like a dove and rest on him". John explicitly announces that Jesus is the one "who baptizes with the Holy Spirit" and John even professes a "belief that he is the Son of God" and "the Lamb of God".

The Gospel of John reports that Jesus' disciples were baptizing and that a debate broke out between some of the disciples of John and another Jew about purification.[[80]](#cite_note-81) In this debate John argued that Jesus "must become greater," while he (John) "must become less."[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83)

The Gospel of John then points out that Jesus' disciples were baptizing more people than John.[[83]](#cite_note-84) Later, the Gospel relates that Jesus regarded John as "a burning and shining lamp, and you were willing to rejoice for a while in his light".[[84]](#cite_note-85)

### Comparative analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Comparative analysis")]

All four Gospels start Jesus' ministry in association with the appearance of John the Baptist.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Simon J. Joseph has argued that the Gospel demotes the historical John by depicting him as a prophetic forerunner to Jesus whereas his ministry actually complemented Jesus'.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

#### The prophecy of Isaiah

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The prophecy of Isaiah")]

Although the Gospel of Mark implies that the arrival of John the Baptist is the fulfilment of a prophecy from the [Book of Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah"), the words quoted ("I will send my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way – a voice of one calling in the wilderness, 'Prepare the way for the Lord, make straight paths for him.'") are a composite of texts from [Isaiah](/wiki/Second_Isaiah "Second Isaiah"), [Malachi](/wiki/Book_of_Malachi "Book of Malachi") and the [Book of Exodus](/wiki/Book_of_Exodus "Book of Exodus"). Matthew and Luke do not include the first part of the reference.[[48]](#cite_note-Carl_R-49)

#### Baptism of Jesus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Baptism of Jesus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Limestone_panel_with_St_John_the_Baptist_in_relief._Early_17th_century.jpg/330px-Limestone_panel_with_St_John_the_Baptist_in_relief._Early_17th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Limestone_panel_with_St_John_the_Baptist_in_relief._Early_17th_century.jpg)

Limestone relief of John the Baptist from [Zakynthos](/wiki/Zakynthos "Zakynthos"), [Byzantine and Christian Museum](/wiki/Byzantine_and_Christian_Museum "Byzantine and Christian Museum"), Greece

The gospels differ on the details of the Baptism. In Mark and Luke, it is Jesus who sees the heavens open and hears a voice address him personally, saying, "You are my dearly loved son; you bring me great joy". They do not clarify whether others saw and heard these things. Although other incidents where the "voice came out of heaven" are recorded in which, for the sake of the crowds, it was heard audibly, John did say in his witness that he did see the spirit coming down "out of heaven" (John 12:28–30, John 1:32).

In Matthew, the voice from heaven does not address Jesus personally, saying instead "This is my beloved son, in whom I am well pleased."

In the Gospel of John, John the Baptist sees the spirit descend as a dove, testifying about the experience as evidence of Jesus's status.

#### John's knowledge of Jesus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: John's knowledge of Jesus")]

John's knowledge of Jesus varies across gospels. In the Gospel of Mark, John preaches of a coming leader, but shows no signs of recognizing that Jesus is this leader. In Matthew, however, John immediately recognizes Jesus and John questions his own worthiness to baptize Jesus. In both Matthew and Luke, John later dispatches disciples to question Jesus about his status, asking "Are you he who is to come, or shall we look for another?" In Luke, John is a familial relative of Jesus whose birth was foretold by Gabriel. In the Gospel of John, John the Baptist himself sees the spirit descend like a dove and he explicitly preaches that Jesus is the Son of God.

#### John and Elijah

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: John and Elijah")]

See also: [Matthew 3:4](/wiki/Matthew_3:4 "Matthew 3:4")

The Gospels vary in their depiction of John's relationship to [Elijah](/wiki/Elijah "Elijah"). Matthew and Mark describe John's attire in a way reminiscent of the description of Elijah in [2 Kings 1:8](/wiki/2_Kings_1:8 "2 Kings 1:8"), who also wore a garment of hair and a leather belt. In Matthew, Jesus explicitly teaches that John is "Elijah who was to come" (Matthew 11:14 – see also Matthew 17:11–13); many Christian theologians have taken this to mean that John was Elijah's successor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the Gospel of John, John the Baptist explicitly denies being Elijah. In the annunciation narrative in Luke, an angel appears to Zechariah, John's father, and tells him that John "will turn many of the sons of Israel to the Lord their God," and that he will go forth "in the spirit and power of Elijah."[[87]](#cite_note-88)
