## In Josephus's *Antiquities of the Jews*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: In Josephus's Antiquities of the Jews")]

An account of John the Baptist is found in all extant manuscripts of the *[Antiquities of the Jews](/wiki/Antiquities_of_the_Jews "Antiquities of the Jews")* (book 18, chapter 5, 2) by [Flavius Josephus](/wiki/Flavius_Josephus "Flavius Josephus") (37–100):[[90]](#cite_note-91)

> Now some of the Jews thought that the destruction of [Herod](/wiki/Herod_Antipas "Herod Antipas")'s [Antipas's] army came from God, and that very justly, as a punishment of what he did against John, that was called the Baptist: for Herod slew him, who was a good man, and commanded the Jews to exercise virtue, both as to righteousness towards one another, and piety towards God, and so to come to baptism; for that the washing [with water] would be acceptable to him, if they made use of it, not in order to the putting away [or the remission] of some sins [only], but for the purification of the body; supposing still that the soul was thoroughly purified beforehand by righteousness. Now when [many] others came in crowds about him, for they were very greatly moved [or pleased] by hearing his words, Herod, who feared lest the great influence John had over the people might put it into his power and inclination to raise a rebellion, (for they seemed ready to do any thing he should advise,) thought it best, by putting him to death, to prevent any mischief he might cause, and not bring himself into difficulties, by sparing a man who might make him repent of it when it would be too late. Accordingly he was sent a prisoner, out of Herod's suspicious temper, to [Macherus](/wiki/Machaerus "Machaerus"), the castle I before mentioned, and was there put to death. Now the Jews had an opinion that the destruction of this army was sent as a punishment upon Herod, and a mark of God's displeasure to him.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

According to this passage, the execution of John was blamed for the defeat Herod suffered. Some have claimed that this passage indicates that John died near the time of the destruction of Herod's army in AD 36. However, in a different passage, Josephus states that the end of Herod's marriage with Aretas's daughter (after which John was killed) was only the beginning of hostilities between Herod and Aretas, which later escalated into the battle.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

Biblical scholar [John Dominic Crossan](/wiki/John_Dominic_Crossan "John Dominic Crossan") differentiates between Josephus's account of John and Jesus, saying, "John had a monopoly, but Jesus had a franchise." To get baptized, Crossan writes, a person went only to John; to stop the movement one only needed to stop John (therefore his movement ended with his death). Jesus invited all to come and see how he and his companions had already accepted the government of God, entered it and were living it. Such a communal praxis was not just for himself, but could survive without him, unlike John's movement.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

## Relics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Relics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Nabi_Yahya_Mosque%2C_Sebastia%2C_c._1920.jpg/250px-Nabi_Yahya_Mosque%2C_Sebastia%2C_c._1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nabi_Yahya_Mosque,_Sebastia,_c._1920.jpg)

[Nabi Yahya Mosque](/wiki/Nabi_Yahya_Mosque "Nabi Yahya Mosque"), traditionally held as the burial site of John the Baptist, in [Sebastia](/wiki/Sebastia_(Palestine) "Sebastia (Palestine)"), near [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus")

See also: [Beheading of John the Baptist § Relics](/wiki/Beheading_of_John_the_Baptist#Relics "Beheading of John the Baptist")

Matthew 14:12 records that "his disciples came and took away [John's] body and buried it."[[94]](#cite_note-95) Theologian [Joseph Benson](/wiki/Joseph_Benson "Joseph Benson") refers to a belief that they managed to do so because "it seems that the body had been thrown over the prison walls, without burial, probably by order of Herodias."[[95]](#cite_note-96)

### The fate of his head

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The fate of his head")]

What became of the head of John the Baptist is difficult to determine. Ancient historians [Josephus](/wiki/Josephus "Josephus"), [Nicephorus](/wiki/Nikephoros_Kallistos_Xanthopoulos "Nikephoros Kallistos Xanthopoulos")[[96]](#cite_note-97) and [Symeon Metaphrastes](/wiki/Symeon_Metaphrastes "Symeon Metaphrastes") assumed that [Herodias](/wiki/Herodias "Herodias") had it buried in the fortress of [Machaerus](/wiki/Machaerus "Machaerus").

An [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") tradition holds that, after being buried, the head was discovered by John's followers and was taken to the [Mount of Olives](/wiki/Mount_of_Olives "Mount of Olives"), where it was twice buried and discovered, the latter events giving rise to the Orthodox feast of the [First and Second Finding of the Head of St. John the Baptist](/wiki/Beheading_of_John_the_Baptist#Related_feasts "Beheading of John the Baptist"). Other writers say that it was interred in Herod's palace in Jerusalem; there it was found during the reign of [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great"), and thence secretly taken to [Emesa (modern Homs, in Syria)](/wiki/Homs "Homs"), where it was concealed, the place remaining unknown for years, until it was manifested by [revelation](/wiki/Revelation "Revelation") in 452,[[97]](#cite_note-98) an event celebrated in the Orthodox Church as the [Third Finding](/wiki/Beheading_of_John_the_Baptist#Related_feasts "Beheading of John the Baptist").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/The_shrine_of_John_the_Baptist%2C_Damascus%2C_April_2008.jpg/250px-The_shrine_of_John_the_Baptist%2C_Damascus%2C_April_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_shrine_of_John_the_Baptist,_Damascus,_April_2008.jpg)

Shrine of John the Baptist in the [Umayyad Mosque](/wiki/Umayyad_Mosque "Umayyad Mosque"), which purportedly houses John the Baptist's head

Two Catholic churches and one mosque claim to have the head of John the Baptist: the [Umayyad Mosque](/wiki/Umayyad_Mosque "Umayyad Mosque"), in [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") ([Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria")); the church of [San Silvestro in Capite](/wiki/San_Silvestro_in_Capite "San Silvestro in Capite"), in Rome; and [Amiens Cathedral](/wiki/Amiens_Cathedral "Amiens Cathedral"), in France (the French king would have had it brought from Constantinople after the [Fourth Crusade](/wiki/Fourth_Crusade "Fourth Crusade")). A fourth claim is made by the [Residenz Museum](/wiki/Munich_Residenz "Munich Residenz") in Munich, Germany, which keeps a reliquary containing what the [Wittelsbach](/wiki/Wittelsbach "Wittelsbach") rulers of Bavaria believed to be the head of Saint John.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

### Right hand relics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Right hand relics")]

According to the Christian Arab [Ibn Butlan](/wiki/Ibn_Butlan "Ibn Butlan"), the [church of Cassian](/wiki/Church_of_Cassian "Church of Cassian") in Antioch held the right arm of John the Baptist until it was smuggled to [Chalcedon](/wiki/Chalcedon "Chalcedon") and later to Constantinople.[[99]](#cite_note-100) An [Orthodox Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") [monastery](/wiki/Cetinje_Monastery "Cetinje Monastery") in [Cetinje](/wiki/Cetinje_Monastery "Cetinje Monastery"), [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro"), and the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") Cathedral of [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena"), in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), both claim to have John the Baptist's right arm and hand, with which he baptised Jesus.[[100]](#cite_note-Hecker2020-101)[[101]](#cite_note-Hooper2010-102) According to the Catholic account, in 1464 [Pope Pius II](/wiki/Pope_Pius_II "Pope Pius II") donated what was identified as the right arm and hand of John the Baptist to the Siena Cathedral.[[102]](#cite_note-103) The donation charter identifies the relic as "the arm of blessed John the Baptist. And this is the very arm that baptized the Lord." The relic is displayed on the high altar of the [Siena Cathedral](/wiki/Siena_Cathedral "Siena Cathedral") annually in June.

[Topkapi Palace](/wiki/Topkapi_Palace "Topkapi Palace"), in Istanbul, claims to have John's right hand index finger.[[101]](#cite_note-Hooper2010-102)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Armenian_Chinsurah_2.jpg/250px-Armenian_Chinsurah_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Armenian_Chinsurah_2.jpg)

A [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") [Armenian](/wiki/Armenians_in_India "Armenians in India") kisses the hand of St John the Baptist at [Chinsurah](/wiki/Chinsurah "Chinsurah").

### Various relics and traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Various relics and traditions")]

#### Right hand – St. John the Baptist Church of Chinsurah (India)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Right hand – St. John the Baptist Church of Chinsurah (India)")]

John the Baptist's right hand is allegedly preserved in the Armenian Apostolic Church of St. John at Chinsurah, [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal"), in India, where each year on "Chinsurah Day" in January it blesses the [Armenian Christians](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church") of [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta").

#### Decapitation cloth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Decapitation cloth")]

The decapitation cloth of Saint John, the cloth which covered his head after his execution, is said to be kept at the [Aachen Cathedral](/wiki/Aachen_Cathedral "Aachen Cathedral"), in Germany.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

#### Historic Armenia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Historic Armenia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Surp_Garabed_Vank_%28Hampikian%2C_1923%29.jpg/250px-Surp_Garabed_Vank_%28Hampikian%2C_1923%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surp_Garabed_Vank_(Hampikian,_1923).jpg)

[Saint Karapet Monastery](/wiki/Saint_Karapet_Monastery "Saint Karapet Monastery"), where Armenian tradition holds that his remains were laid to rest by Gregory the Illuminator[[104]](#cite_note-Kharatyan2013-105)[[105]](#cite_note-Avetisyan1979-106)

According to Armenian tradition, the remains of John the Baptist would in some point have been transferred by [Gregory the Illuminator](/wiki/Gregory_the_Illuminator "Gregory the Illuminator") to the [Saint Karapet Armenian Monastery](/wiki/Surb_Karapet_Monastery "Surb Karapet Monastery").[[104]](#cite_note-Kharatyan2013-105)[[105]](#cite_note-Avetisyan1979-106)

#### Bulgaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Bulgaria")]

In 2010, bones were discovered in the ruins of a Bulgarian church in the St. John the Forerunner Monastery (4th–17th centuries) on the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") island of [Sveti Ivan](/wiki/St._Ivan_Island "St. Ivan Island") and two years later, after DNA and radio carbon testing proved the bones belonged to a Middle Eastern man who lived in the first century AD, scientists said that the remains could conceivably have belonged to John the Baptist.[[106]](#cite_note-Than2012-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108) The remains, found in a reliquarium, are presently kept in the Sts. Cyril and Methodius Cathedral in [Sozopol](/wiki/Sozopol "Sozopol").[[106]](#cite_note-Than2012-107)[[108]](#cite_note-109)

#### Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Egypt")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/St_John_the_Baptists_tomb.JPG/250px-St_John_the_Baptists_tomb.JPG)](/wiki/File:St_John_the_Baptists_tomb.JPG)

Tomb of Saint John the Baptist at a [Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria") monastery in [Lower Egypt](/wiki/Lower_Egypt "Lower Egypt"). The bones of Saint John the Baptist were said to have been found here.

The Coptic Orthodox Church also have claimed to hold the relics of Saint John the Baptist. A crypt and relics said to be John's and mentioned in eleventh- and sixteenth-century manuscripts, were discovered in 1969 during restoration of the Church of St. Macarius at the [Monastery of Saint Macarius the Great](/wiki/Monastery_of_Saint_Macarius_the_Great "Monastery of Saint Macarius the Great") in [Scetes](/wiki/Scetes "Scetes"), Egypt.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

#### Nagorno-Karabakh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Nagorno-Karabakh")]

Additional relics are claimed to reside in [Gandzasar Monastery](/wiki/Gandzasar_Monastery "Gandzasar Monastery")'s Cathedral of St. John the Baptist, in [Nagorno-Karabakh](/wiki/Nagorno-Karabakh "Nagorno-Karabakh").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Purported left finger bone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Purported left finger bone")]

The bone of one of John the Baptist's left fingers is said to be at the [Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art](/wiki/Nelson-Atkins_Museum_of_Art "Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art") in [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"). It is held in a Gothic-style [monstrance](/wiki/Monstrance "Monstrance") made of [gilded silver](/wiki/Gilded_silver "Gilded silver") that dates back to fourteenth-century [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony").[[100]](#cite_note-Hecker2020-101)[[110]](#cite_note-111)

#### Halifax, England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Halifax, England")]

Another obscure claim relates to the town of [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax,_West_Yorkshire "Halifax, West Yorkshire") in West Yorkshire, United Kingdom, where, as patron saint of the town, John the Baptist's head appears on the official coat-of-arms.[[111]](#cite_note-112) One legend (among others) bases the etymology of the town's place-name on "halig" (holy) and "fax" (hair), claiming that a relic of the head, or face, of John the Baptist once existed in the town.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

## Religious views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Religious views")]

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Christianity")]

The [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") describe John the Baptist as having a specific role ordained by [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") as forerunner or precursor of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity"), who was the foretold [Messiah](/wiki/Messiah "Messiah"). The New Testament Gospels speak of this role. In Luke 1:17 the role of John is referred to as being "to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just; to make ready a people prepared for the Lord."[[113]](#cite_note-114) In Luke 1:76 as "thou shalt go before the face of the Lord to prepare his ways"[[114]](#cite_note-115) and in Luke 1:77 as being "To give knowledge of salvation unto his people by the remission of their sins."[[115]](#cite_note-116)

There are several passages within the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") which are interpreted by Christians as being [prophetic](/wiki/Prophetic "Prophetic") of John the Baptist in this role. These include a passage in the [Book of Malachi](/wiki/Book_of_Malachi "Book of Malachi") that refers to a prophet who would "prepare the way of the Lord":

> Behold, I will send my messenger, and he shall prepare the way before me: and the Lord, whom ye seek, shall suddenly come to his temple, even the messenger of the covenant, whom ye delight in: behold, he shall come, saith the LORD of hosts.

— Malachi 3:1[[116]](#cite_note-117)

Also at the end of the next chapter in Malachi 4:5–6 it says,

> Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and dreadful day of the LORD: And he shall turn the heart of the fathers to the children, and the heart of the children to their fathers, lest I come and smite the earth with a curse.

The Jews of Jesus' day expected Elijah to come before the Messiah;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] some present day Jews[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] continue to await Elijah's coming as well, as in the Cup of Elijah the Prophet in the [Passover Seder](/wiki/Passover_Seder "Passover Seder"). This is why the disciples ask Jesus in Matthew 17:10, "Why then say the scribes that Elias must first come?"[[117]](#cite_note-118)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]The disciples are then told by Jesus that Elijah came in the person of John the Baptist,

> Jesus replied, "To be sure, Elijah comes and will restore all things. But I tell you, Elijah has already come, and they did not recognize him, but have done to him everything they wished. In the same way the Son of Man is going to suffer at their hands." Then the disciples understood that he was talking to them about John the Baptist.

— Matthew 17:11–13 (see also 11:14: "...if you are willing to accept it, he is Elijah who was to come.")

These passages are applied to John in the [Synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels").[[118]](#cite_note-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120)[[120]](#cite_note-121) But where Matthew specifically identifies John the Baptist as Elijah's spiritual successor,[[121]](#cite_note-122) the gospels of Mark and Luke are silent on the matter. The [Gospel of John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") states that John the Baptist denied that he was Elijah.

> Now this was John's testimony when the Jews of Jerusalem sent priests and Levites to ask him who he was. He did not deny, but confessed freely, "I am not the Christ." They asked him, "Then who are you? Are you Elijah?" He said, "I am not." "Are you the Prophet?" He answered, "No."

— John 1:19–21

#### Influence on Paul

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Influence on Paul")]

Many scholars believe there was contact between the early church in the [Apostolic Age](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age") and what is called the "[Qumran](/wiki/Qumran "Qumran")-[Essene](/wiki/Essene "Essene") community".[[122]](#cite_note-123) The [Dead Sea Scrolls](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Scrolls "Dead Sea Scrolls") were found at Qumran, which the majority of historians and archaeologists identify as an Essene settlement.[[123]](#cite_note-124) John the Baptist is thought to have been either an Essene or "associated" with the community at Khirbet Qumran. According to the [Book of Acts](/wiki/Book_of_Acts "Book of Acts"), Paul met some "disciples of John" in [Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus").[[124]](#cite_note-125)

#### Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Catholic Church")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Cappella_tornabuoni%2C_12%2C_Nascita_di_san_giovanni_battista.jpg/500px-Cappella_tornabuoni%2C_12%2C_Nascita_di_san_giovanni_battista.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cappella_tornabuoni,_12,_Nascita_di_san_giovanni_battista.jpg)

*The Birth of John the Baptist*, a fresco in the [Tornabuoni Chapel](/wiki/Tornabuoni_Chapel "Tornabuoni Chapel") in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence")

The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") commemorates Saint John the Baptist on two feast days:

* 24 June – [Nativity of Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/Nativity_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist "Nativity of Saint John the Baptist")
* 29 August – [Beheading of Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/Beheading_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist "Beheading of Saint John the Baptist")

According to [Frederick Holweck](/wiki/Frederick_Holweck "Frederick Holweck"), at the [Visitation](/wiki/Visitation_(Christianity) "Visitation (Christianity)") of the Blessed Virgin Mary to his mother Elizabeth, as recounted in Luke 1:39–57, John, sensing the presence of his Jesus, upon the arrival of Mary, leaped in the womb of his mother; he was then cleansed from original sin and filled with the grace of God.[[125]](#cite_note-126) In her *Treatise of Prayer*, Saint [Catherine of Siena](/wiki/Catherine_of_Siena "Catherine of Siena") includes a brief altercation with the [Devil](/wiki/Devil_in_Christianity "Devil in Christianity") regarding her fight due to the Devil attempting to lure her with [vanity](/wiki/Vanity "Vanity") and [flattery](/wiki/Flattery "Flattery"). Speaking in the first person, Catherine responds to the Devil with the following words:

> [...] humiliation of yourself, and you answered the Devil with these words: "Wretch that I am! John the Baptist never sinned and was sanctified in his mother's womb. And I have committed so many sins [...]"

— [Catherine of Siena](/wiki/Catherine_of_Siena "Catherine of Siena"), *A Treatise of Prayer*, 1370.[[126]](#cite_note-127)[[127]](#cite_note-128)

#### Eastern Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Eastern Christianity")]

The [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") and [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") faith believe that John was the last of the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") [prophets](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet"), thus serving as a bridge between that period of [revelation](/wiki/Revelation "Revelation") and the [New Covenant](/wiki/New_Covenant "New Covenant"). They also teach that, following his death, John descended into [Hades](/wiki/Christian_views_on_Hades "Christian views on Hades") and there once more preached that Jesus the Messiah was coming, so he was the Forerunner of Christ in death as he had been in life. Eastern Catholic and Orthodox churches will often have an [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of Saint John the Baptist in a place of honour on the [iconostasis](/wiki/Iconostasis "Iconostasis"), and he is frequently mentioned during the [Divine Services](/wiki/Divine_Services "Divine Services"). Every Tuesday throughout the year is dedicated to his memory.

The Eastern Orthodox Church remembers Saint John the Forerunner on six separate feast days, listed here in order in which they occur during the [church year](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_liturgical_calendar "Eastern Orthodox liturgical calendar") (which begins on 1 September):

* 23 September – [Conception of the Honorable Glorious Prophet, Forerunner and Baptist John](/w/index.php?title=Conception_of_the_Honorable_Glorious_Prophet,_Forerunner_and_Baptist_John&action=edit&redlink=1 "Conception of the Honorable Glorious Prophet, Forerunner and Baptist John (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%97%D0%B0%D1%87%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%98%D0%BE%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%B8 "ru:Зачатие Иоанна Предтечи")][[128]](#cite_note-129)[[129]](#cite_note-130)
* 12 October – [Translation](/wiki/Translation "Translation") from [Malta](/wiki/Sovereign_Military_Order_of_Malta "Sovereign Military Order of Malta") to [Gatchina](/wiki/Gatchina "Gatchina"): of [a Particle of the Life Giving Cross](/wiki/True_Cross "True Cross"), [the Filersk Icon of the Mother of God](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Philermos "Our Lady of Philermos"), and the [relic](/wiki/Relic "Relic") of the [Right Hand of John the Baptist](/w/index.php?title=Right_Hand_of_John_the_Baptist&action=edit&redlink=1 "Right Hand of John the Baptist (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%94%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%86%D0%B0_%D0%98%D0%BE%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%9A%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8F "ru:Десница Иоанна Крестителя")][[130]](#cite_note-131)
* 7 January – [Synaxis of the Holy Glorious Prophet, Forerunner and Baptist John](/w/index.php?title=Synaxis_of_the_Holy_Glorious_Prophet,_Forerunner_and_Baptist_John&action=edit&redlink=1 "Synaxis of the Holy Glorious Prophet, Forerunner and Baptist John (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%A1%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%80_%D0%98%D0%BE%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%B8 "ru:Собор Иоанна Предтечи")]. This is John the Baptist's main feast day, immediately after [Theophany](/wiki/Epiphany_(feast) "Epiphany (feast)") on 6 January (7 January also commemorates the transfer of the relic of the right hand of John the Baptist from [Antioch](/wiki/Antioch "Antioch") to [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") in 956)[[131]](#cite_note-132)

20 January In the Serbian Orthodox Church, the feast day of Saint John the Baptist is called "Jovanjdan" and is celebrated on 20 January (according to the Gregorian calendar).
Key points about Jovanjdan:
Name: "Jovanjdan" directly translates to "John's Day" in Serbian.
Significance: This is a major feast day for Serbian Orthodox Christians, often considered a family "Slava" (patron saint day) where families celebrate with a special feast.
Calendar note: While the Gregorian calendar date is 20 January, the Serbian Orthodox Church uses the Julian calendar, which can result in a slightly different date.

* 24 February – [First and second finding of the Honorable Head of the Holy Glorious Prophet, Forerunner, and Baptist of the Lord, John](/w/index.php?title=First_and_second_finding_of_the_Honorable_Head_of_the_Holy_Glorious_Prophet,_Forerunner,_and_Baptist_of_the_Lord,_John&action=edit&redlink=1 "First and second finding of the Honorable Head of the Holy Glorious Prophet, Forerunner, and Baptist of the Lord, John (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%9E%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%B3%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%8B_%D0%98%D0%BE%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%B8 "ru:Обретение главы Иоанна Предтечи")][[132]](#cite_note-133)
* 25 May – [Third Finding of the Honorable Head of the Holy Glorious Prophet, Forerunner and Baptist John](/w/index.php?title=Third_Finding_of_the_Honorable_Head_of_the_Holy_Glorious_Prophet,_Forerunner_and_Baptist_John&action=edit&redlink=1 "Third Finding of the Honorable Head of the Holy Glorious Prophet, Forerunner and Baptist John (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%9E%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%B3%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%8B_%D0%98%D0%BE%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%B8 "ru:Обретение главы Иоанна Предтечи")][[133]](#cite_note-134)
* 24 June – [Nativity of Saint John the Forerunner](/wiki/Nativity_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist#In_Eastern_Christianity "Nativity of Saint John the Baptist")[[134]](#cite_note-135)
* 29 August – The [Beheading of Saint John the Forerunner](/wiki/Beheading_of_Saint_John_the_Forerunner "Beheading of Saint John the Forerunner"), a day of strict fast and abstinence from meat and dairy products and foods containing meat or dairy products.[[135]](#cite_note-136)

In addition to the above, 5 September is the commemoration of [Zacharias](/wiki/Zechariah_(New_Testament_figure) "Zechariah (New Testament figure)") and [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_(Biblical_person) "Elizabeth (Biblical person)"), Saint John's parents.

The [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") observes 12 October as the Transfer of the Right Hand of the Forerunner from [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") to [Gatchina](/wiki/Gatchina "Gatchina") (1799).

#### Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints")]

[The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") teaches that modern revelation confirms the biblical account of John and also makes known additional events in his ministry. According to this belief, John was "ordained by the angel of God" when he was eight days old "to overthrow the kingdom of the Jews" and to prepare a people for the Lord. Latter-day Saints also believe that "he was baptized while yet in his childhood."[[136]](#cite_note-137)

[Joseph Smith](/wiki/Joseph_Smith "Joseph Smith") said: "Let us come into New Testament times – so many are ever praising the Lord and His apostles. We will commence with John the Baptist. When Herod's edict went forth to destroy the young children, John was about six months older than Jesus, and came under this hellish edict, and Zecharias caused his mother to take him into the mountains, where he was raised on locusts and wild honey. When his father refused to disclose his hiding place, and being the officiating high priest at the Temple that year, was slain by Herod's order, between the porch and the altar, as Jesus said."[[137]](#cite_note-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139)

The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints teaches that John the Baptist appeared on the banks of the [Susquehanna River](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River") near [Harmony Township, Susquehanna County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Harmony_Township,_Susquehanna_County,_Pennsylvania "Harmony Township, Susquehanna County, Pennsylvania"), as a resurrected being to Joseph Smith and [Oliver Cowdery](/wiki/Oliver_Cowdery "Oliver Cowdery") on 15 May 1829, and ordained them to the [Aaronic priesthood](/wiki/Aaronic_priesthood_(LDS_Church) "Aaronic priesthood (LDS Church)").[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141) According to the Church's dispensational view of religious history, John's ministry has operated in three dispensations: he was the last of the prophets under the law of Moses; he was the first of the New Testament prophets; and he was sent to restore the Aaronic priesthood in our day (the [dispensation of the fulness of times](/wiki/Dispensation_of_the_fulness_of_times "Dispensation of the fulness of times")). Latter-day Saints believe John's ministry was foretold by two prophets whose teachings are included in the [Book of Mormon](/wiki/Book_of_Mormon "Book of Mormon"): [Lehi](/wiki/Lehi_(Book_of_Mormon) "Lehi (Book of Mormon)")[[141]](#cite_note-142) and his son [Nephi](/wiki/Nephi,_son_of_Lehi "Nephi, son of Lehi").[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144)

#### Unification Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Unification Church")]

The [Unification Church](/wiki/Unification_Church "Unification Church") teaches that God intended John to help Jesus during his public ministry in Judea. In particular, John should have done everything in his power to persuade the Jewish people that Jesus was the Messiah. He was to become Jesus' main disciple and John's disciples were to become Jesus' disciples. Unfortunately, John did not follow Jesus and continued his own way of baptizing people. Moreover, John also denied that he was Elijah when queried by several Jewish leaders,[[144]](#cite_note-145) contradicting Jesus who stated John is Elijah who was to come.[[145]](#cite_note-146) Many Jews therefore could not accept Jesus as the Messiah because John denied being Elijah, as the prophet's appearance was a prerequisite for the Messiah's arrival as stated in Malachi 4:5.[[146]](#cite_note-147) According to the Unification Church, "John the Baptist was in the position of representing Elijah's physical body, making himself identical with Elijah from the standpoint of their mission."

According to Matthew 11:11, Jesus stated "there has not risen one greater than John the Baptist."[[147]](#cite_note-148) However, in referring to John's blocking the way of the Jews' understanding of him as the Messiah, Jesus said "yet he who is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he." John's failure to follow Jesus became the chief obstacle to the fulfilment of Jesus' mission.[[148]](#cite_note-149)[[149]](#cite_note-150)[[150]](#cite_note-151)

#### Syrian-Egyptian Gnosticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Syrian-Egyptian Gnosticism")]

Among the early Judeo-Christian Gnostics the [Ebionites](/wiki/Ebionites "Ebionites") held that John, along with Jesus and [James the Just](/wiki/James_the_Just "James the Just") – all of whom they revered – were vegetarians.[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154)[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156)[[156]](#cite_note-157) [Epiphanius of Salamis](/wiki/Epiphanius_of_Salamis "Epiphanius of Salamis") records that this group had amended their [Gospel of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") – known today as the [Gospel of the Ebionites](/wiki/Gospel_of_the_Ebionites "Gospel of the Ebionites") – to change where John eats "locusts" to read "honey cakes" or "[manna](/wiki/Manna "Manna")".[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159)

### Mandaeism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Mandaeism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Yahya_Yuhana_Mandi_painting_-_World_of_Light.jpg/250px-Yahya_Yuhana_Mandi_painting_-_World_of_Light.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yahya_Yuhana_Mandi_painting_-_World_of_Light.jpg)

A painting at [Yahya Yuhana Mandi](/wiki/Yahya_Yuhana_Mandi "Yahya Yuhana Mandi") depicting the ascension of Yahya Yuhana (John the Baptist) to the [World of Light](/wiki/World_of_Light "World of Light") during his meeting with [Manda d-Hayyi](/wiki/Manda_d-Hayyi "Manda d-Hayyi"), who appears as a boy. The story is from [Right Ginza](/wiki/Right_Ginza "Right Ginza") Book 5, Chapter 4.

John the Baptist, or Yuhana Maṣbana ([Classical Mandaic](/wiki/Classical_Mandaic_language "Classical Mandaic language"): ࡉࡅࡄࡀࡍࡀ ࡌࡀࡑࡁࡀࡍࡀ, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'John the Baptizer' *Iuhana Maṣbana*)[[15]](#cite_note-Gelbert2011-15) is considered the greatest prophet of the [Mandaeans](/wiki/Mandaeans "Mandaeans"). Mandaeans also refer to him as *Yuhana bar Zakria* (John, son of Zechariah).[[159]](#cite_note-Gelbert2017-160) He plays a large part in their religious texts such as the [Ginza Rabba](/wiki/Ginza_Rabba "Ginza Rabba") and the [Mandaean Book of John](/wiki/Mandaean_Book_of_John "Mandaean Book of John").[[160]](#cite_note-161) Mandaeans believe that they descend directly from John's original disciples[[161]](#cite_note-162) but they do not believe that their religion began with John, tracing their beliefs back to their first prophet Adam.[[44]](#cite_note-Drower-45): 3 According to Mandaeism, John was a great teacher, a [Nasoraean](/wiki/Nazarene_(sect)#Nasoraean_Mandaeans "Nazarene (sect)") and renewer of the faith.[[43]](#cite_note-Buckley-44): 24[[162]](#cite_note-163)[[163]](#cite_note-164) John is a messenger of Light (*nhura*) and Truth (*[kushta](/wiki/Kushta "Kushta")*) who possessed the power of healing and full [Gnosis](/wiki/Gnosis "Gnosis") (*[manda](/wiki/Manda_(Mandaeism) "Manda (Mandaeism)")*).[[164]](#cite_note-165): 48 Mandaean texts make it abundantly clear that early Mandaeans were extremely loyal to John and viewed him as a prophetic reformer of the ancient Mandaean/Israelite tradition.[[165]](#cite_note-166): 108 Scholars such as [Rudolf Macúch](/wiki/Rudolf_Mac%C3%BAch "Rudolf Macúch"), [E. S. Drower](/wiki/E._S._Drower "E. S. Drower"), [Jorunn J. Buckley](/wiki/Jorunn_J._Buckley "Jorunn J. Buckley"), and [Şinasi Gündüz](/wiki/%C5%9Einasi_G%C3%BCnd%C3%BCz "Şinasi Gündüz") believe that the Mandaeans likely have a historical connection with John's original disciples.[[166]](#cite_note-167)[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169)[[43]](#cite_note-Buckley-44) Mandaeans believe that John was married, with his wife named Anhar, and had children.[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171)

Enišbai ([Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_(biblical_figure) "Elizabeth (biblical figure)")) is mentioned as the mother of John the Baptist in chapters 18, 21, and 32 of the [Mandaean Book of John](/wiki/Mandaean_Book_of_John "Mandaean Book of John").[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[159]](#cite_note-Gelbert2017-160)

### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Islam")]

Main article: [John the Baptist in Islam](/wiki/John_the_Baptist_in_Islam "John the Baptist in Islam")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Chester_Beatty_T_414_fol_99r_baby_Yahya_being_held_by_two_angels.jpg/250px-Chester_Beatty_T_414_fol_99r_baby_Yahya_being_held_by_two_angels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chester_Beatty_T_414_fol_99r_baby_Yahya_being_held_by_two_angels.jpg)

Illustration of a 1585-1590 Ottoman manuscript depicting baby Yahya being held by two angels.

In Islam, John the Baptist is known as Yaḥyā ibn Zakariyā ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): يحيى بن زكريا)[[172]](#cite_note-173) and considered the [maternal cousin](/wiki/Cousin "Cousin") of Jesus[[173]](#cite_note-174) as well as a [prophet](/wiki/Prophets_and_messengers_in_Islam "Prophets and messengers in Islam"). He is also believed by [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") to have been a witness to the [word of God](/wiki/Revelation "Revelation") who would herald the coming of Jesus.[[174]](#cite_note-175) His father [Zechariah](/wiki/Zechariah_in_Islam "Zechariah in Islam") was also an Islamic prophet. [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") tradition maintains that John met [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad_in_Islam "Muhammad in Islam") on the night of the [Mi'raj](/wiki/Mi%27raj "Mi'raj"), along with Jesus in the second heaven.[[175]](#cite_note-176) John's story was also told to the [Abyssinian](/wiki/Aksumite_Empire "Aksumite Empire") king during the Muslim refugees' [Migration to Abyssinia](/wiki/Migration_to_Abyssinia "Migration to Abyssinia").[[176]](#cite_note-177) According to the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), John was one on whom God sent peace on the day that he was born and the day that he died.[[177]](#cite_note-178) His tomb is a major pilgrimage site for Muslims in the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus.[[178]](#cite_note-179)

#### Quranic mentions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Quranic mentions")]

The Quran claims that John the Baptist was the first to receive this name (Quran [19:7-10](https://quran.com/19?startingVerse=7)) but since the name Yoḥanan occurs many times before John the Baptist,[[179]](#cite_note-180) this verse is referring either to Islamic scholar consensus that "Yaḥyā" is not the same name as "Yoḥanan"[[180]](#cite_note-181) or to the Biblical account of the miraculous naming of John, which accounted that he was almost named "Zacharias" (Greek: Ζαχαρίας)[[181]](#cite_note-182) after his father's name, as no one in the [lineage](/wiki/Lineage_(anthropology) "Lineage (anthropology)") of his father [Zacharias](/wiki/Zechariah_(priest) "Zechariah (priest)") (also known as [Zechariah](/wiki/Zechariah_(priest) "Zechariah (priest)")) had been named "John" ("Yohanan"/"Yoannes") before him.[[182]](#cite_note-183)

In the Quran, [God](/wiki/God_in_Islam "God in Islam") frequently mentions Zechariah's continuous praying for the birth of a son. Zechariah's wife, mentioned in the New Testament as [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_(Biblical_person) "Elizabeth (Biblical person)") ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): إيشاع) was [barren](/wiki/Infertility "Infertility") and therefore the birth of a child seemed impossible.[[183]](#cite_note-Lives_of_the_Prophets-184) As a gift from God, Zechariah ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): زكريَا) was given a son by the name of "Yaḥya" or "John", a name specially chosen for this child alone. In accordance with Zechariah's prayer, God made John and Jesus, who according to [exegesis](/wiki/Exegesis "Exegesis") was born six months later,[[184]](#cite_note-A-185) renew the message of God, which had been corrupted and lost by the [Israelites](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites"). The Quran says:

> ˹The angels announced,˺ "O Zachariah! Indeed, We give you the good news of ˹the birth of˺ a son, whose name will be John—a name We have not given to anyone before."  
> He wondered, "My Lord! How can I have a son when my wife is barren, and I have become extremely old?"  
> An angel replied, "So will it be! Your Lord says, 'It is easy for Me, just as I created you before, when you were nothing!'"  
> Zachariah said, "My Lord! Grant me a sign." He responded, "Your sign is that you will not ˹be able to˺ speak to people for three nights, despite being healthy."

— [Surah Maryam](/wiki/Maryam_(surah) "Maryam (surah)") [19:7-10](https://quran.com/19?startingVerse=7)

According to the Quran, John was exhorted to hold fast to scripture and was given wisdom by God while still a child.[[185]](#cite_note-186) He was pure and devout, and walked well in the presence of God. He was dutiful towards his parents and he was not arrogant or rebellious. John's reading and understanding of the scriptures, when only a child, surpassed even that of the greatest scholars of the time.[[183]](#cite_note-Lives_of_the_Prophets-184) Muslim [exegesis](/wiki/Exegesis "Exegesis") narrates that Jesus sent John out with twelve disciples,[[186]](#cite_note-187) who preached the message before Jesus called his own disciples.[[184]](#cite_note-A-185) The Quran says:

> ˹It was later said,˺ "O John! Hold firmly to the Scriptures." And We granted him wisdom while ˹he was still˺ a child,

— [Surah Maryam](/wiki/Maryam_(surah) "Maryam (surah)") [19:12](https://quran.com/19?startingVerse=12)

John was a classical prophet,[[187]](#cite_note-188) who was exalted high by God for his bold denouncing of all things sinful. Furthermore, the Quran speaks of John's gentle piety and love and his humble attitude towards life, for which he was granted the Purity of Life:

> as well as purity and compassion from Us. And he was God-fearing,  
> and kind to his parents. He was neither arrogant nor disobedient.  
> Peace be upon him the day he was born, and the day of his death, and the day he will be raised back to life.

— [Surah Maryam](/wiki/Maryam_(surah) "Maryam (surah)") [19:13-15](https://quran.com/19?startingVerse=13)

John is also honoured highly in [Sufism](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism"), primarily because of the Quran's description of John's chastity and kindness.[[188]](#cite_note-189) [Sufis](/wiki/Sufi "Sufi") have frequently applied commentaries on the passages on John in the Quran, primarily concerning the God-given gift of "Wisdom" which he acquired in youth as well as his parallels with Jesus. Although several phrases used to describe John and Jesus are virtually identical in the Quran, the manner in which they are expressed is different.[[189]](#cite_note-190)

### Druze view

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Druze view")]

Druze tradition honours several "mentors" and "prophets", and John the Baptist is honoured as a prophet.[[190]](#cite_note-191) [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") venerate John the Baptist and he is considered a central figure in [Druzism](/wiki/Druzism "Druzism").[[191]](#cite_note-Swayd2015-192) Druze, like some Christians, believe that [Elijah](/wiki/Elijah "Elijah") (al-[Khidr](/wiki/Khidr "Khidr")) came back as John the Baptist,[[191]](#cite_note-Swayd2015-192)[[192]](#cite_note-193) since they believe in [reincarnation](/wiki/Reincarnation "Reincarnation") and the transmigration of the soul.[[193]](#cite_note-194)

### Baháʼí view

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Baháʼí view")]

The [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") considers John to have been a prophet of God who like all other prophets was sent to instill the knowledge of God, promote unity among the people of the world, and to show people the correct way to live.[[194]](#cite_note-195) There are numerous quotations in the writings of [Bahá'u'lláh](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%27u%27ll%C3%A1h "Bahá'u'lláh"), founder of the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"), mentioning John the Baptist. He is regarded by Baháʼís as a [lesser Prophet](/wiki/Manifestation_of_God_(Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith)#Minor_prophets "Manifestation of God (Baháʼí Faith)").[[27]](#cite_note-Compilations1983-28) Bahá'u'lláh claimed that his forerunner, the [Báb](/wiki/B%C3%A1b "Báb"), was the spiritual return of John the Baptist. In his letter to Pope [Pius IX](/wiki/Pius_IX "Pius IX"), Bahá'u'lláh wrote:

> O followers of the Son! We have once again sent John unto you, and He, verily, hath cried out in the wilderness of the [Bayán](/wiki/Persian_Bay%C3%A1n "Persian Bayán"): O peoples of the world! Cleanse your eyes! The Day whereon ye can behold the Promised One and attain unto Him hath drawn nigh! O followers of the Gospel! Prepare the way! The Day of the advent of the Glorious Lord is at hand! Make ready to enter the Kingdom. Thus hath it been ordained by God, He Who causeth the dawn to break.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

John is believed to have had the specific role of foretelling and preparing the way for Jesus. In condemning those who had 'turned aside' from him, Bahá'u'lláh compared them to the followers of John the Baptist, who, he said, "protested against Him Who was the Spirit (Jesus) saying: 'The dispensation of John hath not yet ended; wherefore hast thou come?'" Bahá'u'lláh believed that the Báb played the same role as John in preparing the people for his own coming. As such, Bahá'u'lláh refers to the Báb as 'My Forerunner', the Forerunner being a title that Christians reserve for John the Baptist.[[196]](#cite_note-197) However, Baháʼís consider the [Báb](/wiki/B%C3%A1b "Báb") to be a greater Prophet ([Manifestation of God](/wiki/Manifestation_of_God_(Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith) "Manifestation of God (Baháʼí Faith)")) and thus possessed of a far greater station than John the Baptist.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Scholarship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Scholarship")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Giovanni_di_paolo%2C_scene_della_vita_del_battista%2C_1454%2C_02_partenza_per_il_deserto_1.jpg/500px-Giovanni_di_paolo%2C_scene_della_vita_del_battista%2C_1454%2C_02_partenza_per_il_deserto_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giovanni_di_paolo,_scene_della_vita_del_battista,_1454,_02_partenza_per_il_deserto_1.jpg)

John the Baptist setting off into the desert, by [Giovanni di Paolo](/wiki/Giovanni_di_Paolo "Giovanni di Paolo"), 1454

Scholars studying John the Baptist's relationship with Jesus of Nazareth have commented on the differences in their respective approaches.

[James F. McGrath](/wiki/James_F._McGrath "James F. McGrath") writes "In the first half of the twentieth century, the [Mandaeans](/wiki/Mandaeans "Mandaeans") received significant attention from New Testament scholars who thought that their high view of John the Baptist might mean they were the descendants of his disciples. Many historians think that Jesus of Nazareth was a disciple of John the Baptist before breaking away to form his own movement, and I am inclined to agree."[[197]](#cite_note-198)

[L. Michael White](/wiki/L._Michael_White "L. Michael White") says John the Baptist should be thought of "...primarily as one who was calling for a return to an intensely Jewish piety [...] to follow the way of the Lord [...] to make oneself pure... to be right with God [...] And it seems to be that he calls for baptism as a sign of rededication or repurification of life in a typically Jewish way before God."[[198]](#cite_note-www.pbs.org-199)

[John Dominic Crossan](/wiki/John_Dominic_Crossan "John Dominic Crossan") sees John the Baptist as an apocalyptic eschatologist, whose message was that "God, very soon, imminently, any moment, is going to descend to eradicate the evil of this world in a sort of an apocalyptic consummation..."[[198]](#cite_note-www.pbs.org-199) When Jesus says John is the greatest person ever born on earth, but the least in the Kingdom of God is greater than John, it means Jesus is changing his vision of God and the Kingdom of God from what he has taken from John. For Crossan, Jesus is an ethical eschatologist that sees "...the demand that God is making on us, not us on God so much as God on us, to do something about the evil in the world."[[198]](#cite_note-www.pbs.org-199)

Michael H. Crosby states there was "no biblical evidence indicating that John the Baptist ever became a disciple of Jesus." He believes that John's concept of what a messiah should be was in contrast to how Jesus presented himself, and kept him from becoming a disciple of Jesus. Crosby states, "an unbiased reading leaves us with the figure of John the Baptist as a reformist Jew who also may have wanted desperately to become a believer but was unable to become convinced of Jesus' messiahship..."[[199]](#cite_note-200) Crosby considers John's effectiveness as a "precursor" in encouraging others to follow Jesus as very minimal, since the scriptures record only two of his own followers having become Jesus' disciples. Charles Croll points out that five of John's disciples immediately followed Jesus, four of whom became apostles, one third of the twelve.[[200]](#cite_note-201)

Professor [Candida Moss](/wiki/Candida_Moss "Candida Moss") said that John and Jesus become "de facto competitors in the ancient religious marketplace." After baptizing Jesus, John did not follow Jesus but maintained a separate ministry. After John's death, Jesus' followers had to differentiate him from the executed prophet, "countering the prevalent idea that Jesus was actually John raised from the dead." Moss also references the incident in Matthew 16 where disciples indicated some people believed Jesus was John the Baptist.[[201]](#cite_note-202)

Pastor Robert L. Deffinbaugh views John's sending two of his disciples to ask Jesus if he were the Messiah or whether another should be sought as the Baptist's issuing a public challenge since the message was presented to Jesus while he was with a gathered crowd. Deffinbaugh suggests that John might have been looking for inauguration of the kingdom of God in a more dramatic way than what Jesus was presenting, as John had previously warned that the "Messiah would come with fire." Jesus answered by indicating his miracle works and teachings which themselves gave evidence of his identity: "The blind receive sight, the lame walk, those who have leprosy are cured, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is preached to the poor".[[202]](#cite_note-203)[[203]](#cite_note-204) Charles Croll counters this view, suggesting that John had realized that his time of preparing the way was over and he was saying to Jesus that he now had to testify himself that he was the Coming One. Jesus' affirmative yet indirect response was two fold, first by pointing out that he was doing the work expected of the Messiah, referencing his commissioning text in Isaiah. Secondly, he affirmed John's ministry and message by identifying him as the unshakeable prophet of Malachi 3:1, who was among the greatest people who have ever lived and was not one to bend in the wind (Luke 7:18-35).[[204]](#cite_note-205)

[Harold W. Attridge](/wiki/Harold_W._Attridge "Harold W. Attridge") agrees with Crossan that John was an apocalyptic preacher. Attridge says most contemporary scholars would see the idea of John as the "forerunner" of Jesus as a construct developed by the early church to help explain the relationship between the two. "For the early church it would have been something of an embarrassment to say that Jesus, who was in their minds superior to John the Baptist, had been baptized by him, and thereby proclaimed some sort of subordination to him, some sort of disciple relationship to him..."[[198]](#cite_note-www.pbs.org-199)

[Barbara Thiering](/wiki/Barbara_Thiering "Barbara Thiering") questions the dating of the [Dead Sea Scrolls](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Scrolls "Dead Sea Scrolls") and suggests that the [Teacher of Righteousness](/wiki/Teacher_of_Righteousness "Teacher of Righteousness") (leader of the [Essenes](/wiki/Essenes "Essenes")) preached coming fiery judgement, said "the axe is laid to the roots of the tree", called people "vipers", practised baptism and lived in the wilderness of Judea. Due to these reasons, she believes there is a strong possibility that the Teacher of Righteousness was John the Baptist.[[205]](#cite_note-206) Charles Croll says that the similarities are very superficial and there are many substantial differences between John the Baptist and the Qumran sect.[[206]](#cite_note-207)

## In art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: In art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/John_the_Baptist_by_Prokopiy_Chirin_%281620s%2C_GTG%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-John_the_Baptist_by_Prokopiy_Chirin_%281620s%2C_GTG%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_the_Baptist_by_Prokopiy_Chirin_(1620s,_GTG)_(cropped).jpg)

Eastern Orthodox [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") *John the Baptist – the Angel of the Desert* ([Stroganov school](/wiki/Stroganov_school "Stroganov school"), 1620s) [Tretyakov Gallery](/wiki/Tretyakov_Gallery "Tretyakov Gallery"), Moscow

### Early Christian art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Early Christian art")]

The earliest depictions of St John are found in the *[Baptism of Christ](/wiki/Baptism_of_Christ "Baptism of Christ")*,[[19]](#cite_note-Oxford_University_Press2005-20) one of the earliest scenes from the *[Life of Christ](/wiki/Life_of_Christ_in_art "Life of Christ in art")* to be frequently depicted in [Early Christian art](/wiki/Early_Christian_art "Early Christian art"), and John's tall, thin, even gaunt, and bearded figure is already established by the fifth century. Only he and Jesus are consistently shown with long hair from Early Christian times, when the apostles generally have trim classical cuts; in fact John is more consistently depicted in this way than Jesus.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Byzantine and Eastern Orthodox art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Byzantine and Eastern Orthodox art")]

In Byzantine and later Eastern Orthodox art, John the Baptist and the Holy Virgin Mary often flank Jesus on either side. The composition of the *[Deesis](/wiki/Deesis "Deesis")* came to be included in every [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") church, as remains the case to this day. Here John and the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") (Mary the "God-bearer") flank a [Christ Pantocrator](/wiki/Christ_Pantocrator "Christ Pantocrator") and intercede for humanity.

In Eastern Orthodox [icons](/wiki/Icons "Icons"), he often has angel's wings, since Mark 1:2[[207]](#cite_note-208) describes him as a messenger.[[19]](#cite_note-Oxford_University_Press2005-20)[[208]](#cite_note-Hall-209)

### Western art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Western art")]

After the earliest images showing the Baptism of the Lord follow ones with St John shown as an ascetic wearing camel hair, with a staff and scroll inscribed (in Western art) *"Ecce Agnus Dei"*, or bearing a book or dish with a lamb on it.[[19]](#cite_note-Oxford_University_Press2005-20)

The Baptist is very often shown on altarpieces designed for churches dedicated to him, where the donor was named for him or where there was some other patronage connection. John the Baptist is the patron saint of Florence and has often been depicted in the art of that city,[[209]](#cite_note-210) and also frequently appears in [baptistries](/wiki/Baptistries "Baptistries"), which are very often dedicated to him.[[210]](#cite_note-Hall2-211) Major works depicting St John the Baptist can be found in the [Florence Baptistery](/wiki/Florence_Baptistery "Florence Baptistery"), including the mosaics on the vault, the bronze doors by [Andrea Pisano](/wiki/Andrea_Pisano "Andrea Pisano"), and the great silver altar[[211]](#cite_note-212) now in the [Museo dell'Opera del Duomo](/wiki/Museo_dell%27Opera_del_Duomo_(Florence) "Museo dell'Opera del Duomo (Florence)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/InfantJesus_JohnBaptist.JPG/250px-InfantJesus_JohnBaptist.JPG)](/wiki/File:InfantJesus_JohnBaptist.JPG)

John the Baptist (right) with the [Christ Child](/wiki/Christ_Child "Christ Child"), in *The Holy Children with a Shell* by [Bartolomé Esteban Murillo](/wiki/Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo "Bartolomé Esteban Murillo")

A number of narrative scenes from his life were often shown on the [predella](/wiki/Predella "Predella") of altarpieces dedicated to John, and other settings, notably in the frescoes by Giotto for the [Peruzzi Chapel](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Peruzzi_Chapel "c:Category:Peruzzi Chapel")[[212]](#cite_note-213) in the church of [Santa Croce](/wiki/Santa_Croce,_Florence "Santa Croce, Florence"), the large series in [grisaille](/wiki/Grisaille "Grisaille") fresco in the [Chiostro dello Scalzo](/wiki/Chiostro_dello_Scalzo "Chiostro dello Scalzo"), which was [Andrea del Sarto](/wiki/Andrea_del_Sarto "Andrea del Sarto")'s largest work, and the frescoed *Life* by [Domenico Ghirlandaio](/wiki/Domenico_Ghirlandaio "Domenico Ghirlandaio") in the [Tornabuoni Chapel](/wiki/Tornabuoni_Chapel "Tornabuoni Chapel"), all in Florence. There is another important fresco cycle by [Filippo Lippi](/wiki/Filippo_Lippi "Filippo Lippi") in [Prato Cathedral](/wiki/Prato_Cathedral "Prato Cathedral"). These include the typical scenes:[[213]](#cite_note-214) the Annunciation to [Zechariah](/wiki/Zechariah_(priest) "Zechariah (priest)"); John's birth; his naming by his father; the [Visitation](/wiki/Visitation_(Christianity) "Visitation (Christianity)"); John's departure for the desert; his preaching in the desert; the [Baptism of Christ](/wiki/Baptism_of_Christ "Baptism of Christ"); John before Herod; the dance of Herod's stepdaughter, [Salome](/wiki/Salome "Salome"); [his beheading](/wiki/Beheading_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist "Beheading of Saint John the Baptist"); and the daughter of [Herodias](/wiki/Herodias "Herodias") Salome carrying his head on a platter.[[214]](#cite_note-215)[[215]](#cite_note-216)

His birth, which unlike the [Nativity of Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus_in_art "Nativity of Jesus in art") allowed a relatively wealthy domestic interior to be shown, became increasingly popular as a subject in the late [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"),[[210]](#cite_note-Hall2-211) with depictions by [Jan van Eyck](/wiki/Jan_van_Eyck "Jan van Eyck") in the [Turin-Milan Hours](/wiki/Turin-Milan_Hours "Turin-Milan Hours") and Ghirlandaio in the Tornabuoni Chapel being among the best known. His execution, a church feast-day, was often shown; by the fifteenth century, scenes such as the dance of Salome became popular; sometimes, as in an [engraving](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving") by [Israhel van Meckenem](/wiki/Israhel_van_Meckenem "Israhel van Meckenem"), the interest of the artist is clearly in showing the life of Herod's court, given contemporary dress, as much as the martyrdom of the saint.[[216]](#cite_note-217) The execution was usually by a swordsman, with John kneeling in prayer, Salome often standing by with an empty platter, and Herod and Herodias at table in a cut-through view of a building in the background.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Head_of_St._John_the_Baptist_on_a_Plate%2C_Southern_Netherlands%2C_c._1430%2C_oak_-_Bode-Museum_-_DSC03181_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Head_of_St._John_the_Baptist_on_a_Plate%2C_Southern_Netherlands%2C_c._1430%2C_oak_-_Bode-Museum_-_DSC03181_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Head_of_St._John_the_Baptist_on_a_Plate,_Southern_Netherlands,_c._1430,_oak_-_Bode-Museum_-_DSC03181_(cropped).JPG)

Head of St John the Baptist on a Plate, Southern Netherlands, c. 1430, oak

Salome bearing John's head on a platter equally became a subject for the [Power of Women](/wiki/Power_of_Women "Power of Women") group: a Northern Renaissance fashion for images of glamorous but dangerous women ([Delilah](/wiki/Delilah "Delilah"), [Judith](/wiki/Judith "Judith") and others).[[217]](#cite_note-218) It was often painted by [Lucas Cranach the Elder](/wiki/Lucas_Cranach_the_Elder "Lucas Cranach the Elder") and engraved by the [Little Masters](/wiki/Little_Masters "Little Masters"). When the head is brought to the table by Salome, Herod may be shown as startled, if not disgusted, but Herodias is usually not. These images remained popular into the Baroque, with [Carlo Dolci](/wiki/Carlo_Dolci "Carlo Dolci") painting at least three versions. John preaching, in a landscape setting, was a popular subject in Dutch art from [Pieter Brueghel the Elder](/wiki/Pieter_Brueghel_the_Elder "Pieter Brueghel the Elder") and his successors.[[218]](#cite_note-219) The isolated motif of the severed head, often on its platter, was a frequent image, often in sculpture, from the late Middle Ages onwards,[[219]](#cite_note-Hall3-220) known as *Ioannes in disco* (Latin for "John on a plate").

As a child (of varying age), he is sometimes shown from the fifteenth century in family scenes from the life of Christ such as the [Holy Family](/wiki/Holy_Family#In_art "Holy Family"),[[220]](#cite_note-221) the [Presentation of Christ](/wiki/Presentation_of_Christ "Presentation of Christ"), the [Marriage of the Virgin](/wiki/Marriage_of_the_Virgin "Marriage of the Virgin") and the [Holy Kinship](/wiki/Holy_Kinship "Holy Kinship"). In the Baptism of Christ his presence was obligatory.[[221]](#cite_note-222) [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s two versions of the *[Virgin of the Rocks](/wiki/Virgin_of_the_Rocks "Virgin of the Rocks")* were influential in establishing a Renaissance fashion for variations on the [Madonna and Child](/wiki/Madonna_and_Child "Madonna and Child") which included John. [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael") in particular painted many compositions of the subject, such as the *[Alba Madonna](/wiki/Alba_Madonna "Alba Madonna")*, *[La belle jardinière](/wiki/La_belle_jardini%C3%A8re "La belle jardinière")*, the *[Garvagh Madonna](/wiki/Garvagh_Madonna "Garvagh Madonna")*, the *[Madonna della seggiola](/wiki/Madonna_della_seggiola "Madonna della seggiola")*, and the *[Madonna dell'Impannata](/wiki/Madonna_dell%27Impannata "Madonna dell'Impannata")*, which are among his best-known works.

John was also often shown by himself as an adolescent or adult, usually already wearing his distinctive dress and carrying a long thin wooden cross[[208]](#cite_note-Hall-209) – another theme [influenced by Leonardo](/wiki/Saint_John_the_Baptist_(Leonardo) "Saint John the Baptist (Leonardo)"), whose equivocal composition, with the camel-skin dress, was developed by Raphael, [Titian](/wiki/Titian "Titian") and [Guido Reni](/wiki/Guido_Reni "Guido Reni") among many others. Often he is accompanied by a lamb, especially in the many [Early Netherlandish paintings](/wiki/Early_Netherlandish_painting "Early Netherlandish painting") which needed this attribute as he wore normal clothes, or a red robe over a not very clearly indicated camel skin.[[208]](#cite_note-Hall-209) [Caravaggio](/wiki/Caravaggio "Caravaggio") painted an especially large number of works depicting John, from [at least five largely nude youths](/wiki/John_the_Baptist_(Caravaggio) "John the Baptist (Caravaggio)") attributed to him, to three late works on his death – the great *[Execution](/wiki/The_Beheading_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist_(Caravaggio) "The Beheading of Saint John the Baptist (Caravaggio)")* in Malta, and two sombre Salomes with his head, [one in Madrid](/wiki/Salome_with_the_Head_of_John_the_Baptist_(Madrid)_(Caravaggio) "Salome with the Head of John the Baptist (Madrid) (Caravaggio)"), and [one in London](/wiki/Salome_with_the_Head_of_John_the_Baptist_(Caravaggio,_London) "Salome with the Head of John the Baptist (Caravaggio, London)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/John_Everett_Millais_-_Christ_in_the_House_of_His_Parents_%28%60The_Carpenter%27s_Shop%27%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/500px-John_Everett_Millais_-_Christ_in_the_House_of_His_Parents_%28%60The_Carpenter%27s_Shop%27%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Everett_Millais_-_Christ_in_the_House_of_His_Parents_(%60The_Carpenter%27s_Shop%27)_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

St John (right) in *[Christ in the House of His Parents](/wiki/Christ_in_the_House_of_His_Parents "Christ in the House of His Parents")* by [John Everett Millais](/wiki/John_Everett_Millais "John Everett Millais"), 1849–50

[Amiens Cathedral](/wiki/Amiens_Cathedral "Amiens Cathedral"), which holds one of the alleged heads of the Baptist, has a biographical sequence in polychrome relief, dating from the sixteenth century. This includes the execution and the disposal of the saint's remains, which according to legend were burnt in the reign of [Julian the Apostate](/wiki/Julian_the_Apostate "Julian the Apostate") (fourth century) to prevent pilgrimages.[[219]](#cite_note-Hall3-220)

A remarkable [Pre-Raphaelite](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite "Pre-Raphaelite") portrayal is *[Christ in the House of His Parents](/wiki/Christ_in_the_House_of_His_Parents "Christ in the House of His Parents")* by [John Everett Millais](/wiki/John_Everett_Millais "John Everett Millais"). Here the Baptist is shown as a child, wearing a loin covering of animal skins, hurrying into [Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph")'s carpenter shop with a bowl of water to join [Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus"), Joseph, and Mary's mother [Anne](/wiki/Saint_Anne "Saint Anne") in soothing the injured hand of Jesus. Artistic interest enjoyed a considerable revival at the end of the nineteenth century with [Symbolist](/wiki/Symbolist "Symbolist") painters such as [Gustave Moreau](/wiki/Gustave_Moreau "Gustave Moreau") and [Puvis de Chavannes](/wiki/Puvis_de_Chavannes "Puvis de Chavannes") ([National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery"), London).[[222]](#cite_note-223) [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde")'s play *[Salome](/wiki/Salome_(play) "Salome (play)")* was illustrated by [Aubrey Beardsley](/wiki/Aubrey_Beardsley "Aubrey Beardsley"), giving rise to some of his most memorable images.

* [![St. John the Baptist (c. 1513–1516), Leonardo da Vinci](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_St_John_the_Baptist_-_WGA12723.jpg/120px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_St_John_the_Baptist_-_WGA12723.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_St_John_the_Baptist_-_WGA12723.jpg "St. John the Baptist (c. 1513–1516), Leonardo da Vinci")

### In poetry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: In poetry")]

The [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") poet [Lucrezia Tornabuoni](/wiki/Lucrezia_Tornabuoni "Lucrezia Tornabuoni") chose John the Baptist as one of the biblical figures on which she wrote poetry.[[223]](#cite_note-224)

He is also referenced in "[The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock](/wiki/The_Love_Song_of_J._Alfred_Prufrock "The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock")" by [T. S. Eliot](/wiki/T._S._Eliot "T. S. Eliot") in stanza 12.

### In music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: In music")]

* [Guido D'Arezzo](/wiki/Guido_D%27Arezzo "Guido D'Arezzo") (991/992 – after 1033) an Italian Benedictine monk founded the standard music stave based on a hymn to Saint John the Baptist. The hymn that begins with [Ut Queant Laxis](/wiki/Ut_Queant_Laxis "Ut Queant Laxis") uses the first syllable for each line – Ut (later changed to Do), Re, Mi, Fa, Sol, La, Si. The teaching is also known as the [solmization](/wiki/Solmization "Solmization") syllable.[[224]](#cite_note-225)
* *[This is the Record of John](/wiki/This_is_the_Record_of_John "This is the Record of John")*, by [English](/wiki/England "England") [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_Period "Tudor Period") composer [Orlando Gibbons](/wiki/Orlando_Gibbons "Orlando Gibbons") is a well-known part-setting of the [Gospel of John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") for solo voice, choir and [organ](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ") or [viol](/wiki/Viol "Viol") accompaniment.[[225]](#cite_note-226)
* The reformer [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") wrote a [hymn](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") based on biblical accounts about the Baptist, "*[Christ unser Herr zum Jordan kam](/wiki/Christ_unser_Herr_zum_Jordan_kam "Christ unser Herr zum Jordan kam")*" (1541), based for a cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach for the [feast day on 24 June](/wiki/Nativity_of_St_John_the_Baptist "Nativity of St John the Baptist"), [*Christ unser Herr zum Jordan kam*, BWV 7](/wiki/Christ_unser_Herr_zum_Jordan_kam,_BWV_7 "Christ unser Herr zum Jordan kam, BWV 7") (1724).
* [*S. Giovanni Battista*](/w/index.php?title=S._Giovanni_Battista&action=edit&redlink=1 "S. Giovanni Battista (page does not exist)") [[scores](https://imslp.org/wiki/S._Giovanni_Battista,_G.3.3_(Stradella,_Alessandro) "scores:S. Giovanni Battista, G.3.3 (Stradella, Alessandro)")] (St. John the Baptist) is a 1676 [oratorio](/wiki/Oratorio "Oratorio") by [Alessandro Stradella](/wiki/Alessandro_Stradella "Alessandro Stradella").
* The well-known Advent hymn *On Jordan's Bank the Baptist's cry* was written by [Charles Coffin](/wiki/Charles_Coffin_(writer) "Charles Coffin (writer)").[[226]](#cite_note-227)
* John the Baptist (Jokanaan), Baritone, is a character in the opera [Salome](/wiki/Salome_(opera) "Salome (opera)") by [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss"), premiered 1905 in Dresden. The text is from [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde")'s French play, translated into German by [Hedwig Lachmann](/wiki/Hedwig_Lachmann "Hedwig Lachmann").[[227]](#cite_note-228)
* In popular music, [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") dedicates four lines to John the Baptist in "[Tombstone Blues](/wiki/Tombstone_Blues "Tombstone Blues")", the second track of his 1965 album *[Highway 61 Revisited](/wiki/Highway_61_Revisited "Highway 61 Revisited")*. He sings: "John the Baptist after torturing a thief/Looks up at his hero the Commander-in-Chief/Saying, "Tell me great hero, but please make it brief/Is there a hole for me to get sick in?".[[228]](#cite_note-229)
* The song "John the Baptist (Holy John)" by [Al Kooper](/wiki/Al_Kooper "Al Kooper") on his 1971 album *[New York City (You're a Woman)](/wiki/New_York_City_(You%27re_a_Woman) "New York City (You're a Woman)")* is about John the Baptist. In the same year the song was also recorded by [Blood, Sweat & Tears](/wiki/Blood,_Sweat_%26_Tears "Blood, Sweat & Tears") for their album *[Blood, Sweat & Tears 4](/wiki/Blood,_Sweat_%26_Tears_4 "Blood, Sweat & Tears 4")*.
* In his song "Everyman Needs a Companion", the closing track to his album *[Fear Fun](/wiki/Fear_Fun "Fear Fun")*, [Father John Misty](/wiki/Father_John_Misty "Father John Misty") sings about the friendship between John the Baptist and Jesus of Nazareth: "John the Baptist took Jesus Christ/Down to the river on a Friday night/They talked about Mary like a couple of boys/With nothing to lose/Too scared to try."[[229]](#cite_note-230)
* John the Baptist is referenced in the music of American [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") band [Om](/wiki/Om_(band) "Om (band)") in their 2009 song "Meditation Is the Practice of Death".[[230]](#cite_note-231) As well as this, John the Baptist is depicted on the cover art of Om's 2012 album, *[Advaitic Songs](/wiki/Advaitic_Songs "Advaitic Songs")*.

### In film and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_the_Baptist&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: In film and television")]

John the Baptist has appeared in a number of screen adaptations of the life of Jesus. Actors who have played John include James D. Ainsley in *[From the Manger to the Cross](/wiki/From_the_Manger_to_the_Cross "From the Manger to the Cross")* (1912), [Nigel De Brulier](/wiki/Nigel_De_Brulier "Nigel De Brulier") in *[Salome](/wiki/Salome_(1923_film) "Salome (1923 film)")* (1923), [Alan Badel](/wiki/Alan_Badel "Alan Badel") in *[Salome](/wiki/Salome_(1953_film) "Salome (1953 film)")* (1953), [Robert Ryan](/wiki/Robert_Ryan "Robert Ryan") in *[King of Kings](/wiki/King_of_Kings_(1961_film) "King of Kings (1961 film)")* (1961), Mario Socrate in *[The Gospel According to St. Matthew](/wiki/The_Gospel_According_to_St._Matthew_(film) "The Gospel According to St. Matthew (film)")* (1964), [Charlton Heston](/wiki/Charlton_Heston "Charlton Heston") in *[The Greatest Story Ever Told](/wiki/The_Greatest_Story_Ever_Told "The Greatest Story Ever Told")* (1965), [David Haskell](/wiki/David_Haskell "David Haskell") in *[Godspell](/wiki/Godspell_(film) "Godspell (film)")* (1973),
